cataloging_subject.label: The Earl of March to Henry IV
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The cataloged subject is the single letter written at Dunbar by George Dunbar, Earl of March, to Henry IV on 18 February 1400, including its Middle French address endorsement. CME00126 also represents F. C. Hingeston's supplied heading, marginal summaries, witness note, footnotes, and signature identification as a distinct editorial layer. It is not Hingeston's 1860 volume as a whole, Cotton MS Vespasian F VII as a codex, the physical paper document at f. 22, the University of Michigan encoding, the Internet Archive scan, or the checked-in XML artifact.

catalog_summary.title: The Earl of March to Henry IV
catalog_summary.author: George Dunbar, Earl of March

identifiers.value: Cotton MS Vespasian F VII, f. 22

titles.value: The Earl of March to Henry IV

assertion.notes: An established editorial access title, not a medieval title written on the letter.

titles.value: Letter from George Dunbar, Earl of March, to Henry IV (18 February 1400)

agents.name: George Dunbar, Earl of March
alternate_names: Le Count de la Marche d'Escoce
notes: The preferred name deliberately omits an ordinal. Hingeston calls the writer the eleventh earl, while the current University of Edinburgh archive authority for George Dunbar (1338-1420) calls him the tenth Earl of Dunbar and March.

assertion.notes: The signature uses the French title; the current BL catalog identifies the sender as Geo. Dunbar, Earl of March. No inspected source attributes the address endorsement's hand or wording to Dunbar personally.

scope.description: The 1860 transcription, supplied heading, marginal summaries, notes, and chronological placement

date_statements.display: 18 February 1400 (day and month explicit; year supplied by Hingeston's chronological catalogue and the current British Library catalog)

assertion.notes: The letter's dateline gives the eighteenth day of February but no year. Hingeston's chronological catalogue explicitly assigns 18 February 1400 after weighing internal evidence, and the current BL catalog independently records Dunbar, Feb. 18, 1400.
Hingeston is the earliest publication established in this research, not a proof that no earlier printing exists.
The letter's dateline says it was written at the writer's castle of Dunbar.

language_statements.label: Older Scots

assertion.notes: More specifically Early Scots under DSL's periodization (up to about 1450). The letter itself calls its vernacular 'Englishe'; DSL explains that Scots was called Inglis before 1494. U-M and the legacy XML normalize the text as Middle English (enm), which is retained as representation history rather than preferred work-level language cataloging.

notes: The normalized work-level code is sco. Modern English headings and notes belong to Hingeston's editorial layer and are not promoted to a work-level language facet.

form_statements.description: A prose diplomatic letter with a brief prose address endorsement and nineteenth-century prose editorial apparatus.

assertion.notes: Dunbar complains of a broken marriage agreement and requests Henry's aid and a safe-conduct while offering service.

subject_statements.label: Anglo-Scottish relations, claims, geography, and military planning

assertion.notes: The shared corpus vocabulary label is broader than this individual letter; CME00126 chiefly concerns allegiance, cross-border access, and political aid.

summaries.value: George Dunbar, Earl of March, tells Henry IV that the Duke of Rothesay wronged his daughter by marrying another woman despite a sealed obligation. Dunbar offers service to Henry, asks for a trusted intermediary and a safe-conduct for one hundred knights, squires, and servants, explains their asserted kinship, and requests aid. He closes from Dunbar on 18 February; a Middle French endorsement addresses the king of England.

CME00126 encodes Hingeston's complete letter X from the heading in the lower half of printed p. 23 through the endorsement near the top of p. 25. It excludes the preceding letter and translation above it on p. 23 and letter XI beginning below it on p. 25. Hingeston's title, witness note, marginal summaries, cross-references, date gloss, and earl-identification note are represented as editorial apparatus rather than medieval letter text.

The XML has one letter DIV1, two HEAD elements, eight NOTE elements, eight P elements, and four PB elements. Seven P elements form the main letter and one P is the endorsement tailnote. The initial unnumbered PB is administrative; numbered PBs map printed pp. 23-25. The XML's Rymer footnote reads viii. 181, while the inspected 1860 source page reads viii. 131.

extent.description: Twelve cataloged prose structural items: one numbered letter work, one address endorsement, two editorial HEAD elements, and eight editorial NOTE elements, all in one DIV1; the letter body contains seven main P elements plus a closer, and numbered page breaks map printed pp. 23-25.

content_parts.label: Letter from George Dunbar to Henry IV
content_parts.summary: Salutation, complaint over the Duke of Rothesay's broken marriage obligation, offer of political service, requests for an intermediary, safe-conduct, and aid, kinship explanation, blessing, dateline, and signature.

locators.value: f. 22; exact written side or sides not publicly verified

content_parts.label: Middle French address endorsement
content_parts.summary: Address to the most excellent, powerful, and noble prince, the king of England.

locators.value: p. 25, immediately after the signature and before letter XI

content_parts.label: Hingeston editorial apparatus
content_parts.summary: Supplied item number and title, witness and cross-reference note, four marginal summaries, safe-conduct and feast-date footnotes, and identification of the signatory.

locators.value: pp. 23-25, interleaved headings, margins, and footnotes
two HEAD and eight NOTE elements

notes: The count comprises two HEAD plus eight NOTE elements.
Modern English editorial prose is not treated as an intellectual-work language facet.
Hingeston's earl ordinal and the Rymer reference discrepancy remain attributed editorial claims rather than silently normalized facts.

evidence.citation: Local structural and textual audit of CME00126.xml against Hingeston's 1860 page images.
evidence.summary: Counted one letter DIV1, two HEAD, eight NOTE, eight P, and four PB elements; verified the exact printed-unit boundary and the source/XML Rymer 131/181 discrepancy.

evidence.citation: F. C. Hingeston, ed., Royal and Historical Letters During the Reign of Henry the Fourth, vol. 1 (London: Longman, Green, Longman, and Roberts, 1860), letter X, pp. 23-25; chronological catalogue p. xcviii; preface pp. xiii-xiv.
evidence.locator: Printed pp. 23-25; physical PDF pp. 143-145; BookReader leaves n143-n145; catalogue PDF p. 106; preface PDF pp. 21-22
evidence.summary: Exact source edition, visually inspected for the title page, complete letter boundary, text and apparatus, witness statement, source reference Rymer viii. 131, explicit chronological-catalogue date 18 February 1400, and editorial method.

evidence.citation: British Library, Cotton MS Vespasian F VII, record 040-001103462.
evidence.summary: Current official catalog evidence identifying the original letter from Geo. Dunbar, Earl of March, to Henry IV at f. 22 and dating it Dunbar, 18 February 1400; also records codex support, extent, dimensions, foliation, condition, binding, and access restrictions.
notes: The hash is for the exact cached JSON representation of record 040-001103462.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library, 'The Earl of March to Henry IV,' CME00126.
evidence.summary: Official item page confirming the established title, Hingeston source citation, public electronic text, provider author string, and No Copyright - United States rights statement.

evidence.citation: Jeremy Smith, 'Scots: an outline history,' Dictionaries of the Scots Language (2017), Origins and Dominance of Scots.
evidence.summary: Scholarly language-history evidence that Older Scots includes Early Scots to about 1450, continuous written records begin in 1375-76, and writers called the language Inglis before 1494.

evidence.citation: Middle English Dictionary, entry til, quotations from '(1400) Let.Hen.IV in RS 18.1 (Vsp F.7),' p. 24.
evidence.summary: Current scholarly citation corroborating the conventional 1400 date, Rolls Series 18.1 source, Vespasian F VII siglum, and use of the letter as Middle English lexicographic evidence.

evidence.citation: University of Edinburgh Library Heritage Collections, La.V/29, creator authority 'Dunbar, George, 1338-1420 (10th Earl of Dunbar and March).'
evidence.summary: Current institutional authority evidence identifying the relevant George Dunbar as the tenth Earl of Dunbar and March, in conflict with Hingeston's eleventh-earl note.

open_questions.question: Which ordinal convention should be used for George Dunbar in this record: Hingeston's eleventh Earl or the current University of Edinburgh authority's tenth Earl of Dunbar and March?
next_steps: Consult a current specialist peerage history or an authoritative national name authority that explicitly explains the numbering convention.
Keep the preferred name ordinal-free until the convention is documented.
notes: The conflict may reflect whether Dunbar and March successions are counted separately or an older genealogical convention; no resolution is inferred here.

open_questions.question: Should the CME XML footnote 'See Rymer, viii. 181' be corrected to the source edition's 'See Rymer, viii. 131'?
next_steps: Verify the cited Foedera passage directly.
If confirmed, correct the XML in a separately scoped encoding change while preserving this manifest's description of the current artifact.
notes: The current U-M full-text display also reads 181; the discrepancy is not silently repaired in metadata.
Henry IV is the recipient and a subject of the letter, not a creator responsibility under the available schema.
The date's day and month are internal; the year is a high-confidence supplied date supported by both Hingeston's explicit chronological catalogue and the current BL record.
The Middle French endorsement is part of the transmitted document, but its hand and personal wording responsibility are not identified by the inspected sources.
The modern editorial title and notes are represented as apparatus rather than extra intellectual-work language facets.
